lesson.menu_title: The Elevator Pitch -- 90 Seconds That Count
lesson.menu_desc: Build a short, punchy pitch -- hook, what you do, the value, and the ask -- and say WHY it matters with to and so that -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 -- The Elevator Pitch, 90 Seconds That Count
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Diogo Leal | Aula 17 -- The Elevator Pitch

listenings.text: Hi Diogo, it is Sophia. Last class you delivered a full, polished talk. Now we go shorter, much shorter. An elevator pitch is ninety seconds, or less. You meet someone important in an elevator, at a coffee break, or in a hallway, and you have one short moment to tell them who you are and why your work matters. A good pitch has four small parts. First, a hook, one line that grabs attention. Second, what you do, in plain words. Third, the value, the real benefit you bring. And fourth, the ask, one clear next step, like a meeting or a call. Keep it simple. Always say why your work matters, not only what you do. Say, I automate reports so that managers save two hours a day. That little word, so that, turns a task into a benefit. Practice your pitch out loud until it is smooth and natural. You never know who you will meet in the elevator. Talk soon.

Hi, I am Diogo Leal. Have you ever lost a whole afternoon to a report that a computer could write for you? I am an IT project manager at Oracle. I build small tools to remove that kind of slow, manual work. For example, last quarter I automated the monthly status report so that managers save two hours every week. The real value is simple. Teams stop copying numbers by hand and they spend that time on real decisions. I would love fifteen minutes to show you how the same idea could help your team. Could we set up a short call next week? Here is my card. It was great to meet you.

extra_audio.text: Diogo gives a short, ninety-second pitch in the elevator. First, he opens with a hook, one line that grabs attention. Then, he says what he does in plain words: he builds small tools to remove slow, manual work. After that, he gives the value, the real benefit: managers save two hours every week. Finally, he makes the ask, one clear next step: he asks for a short call next week and hands over his card.
